name: Harvest Circle
identity: Cultural and market town where harvest patron-families, quota clerks, and festival brokers turn field output into alliance redistribution.
approach: Open market rings, wagon circles, drying racks, and shrine lawns announce a town built for exchange as much as storage.
inside: Granaries, counting courts, communal tables, festival grounds, and family compounds share one busy civic center.
who_is_out_of_place: people who insult local rites or cut market lines
expected_behavior: respect family-controlled stalls and convoy order
treat festival exchange as civic duty as well as profit
faith: unity and harvest rites are public, crowded, and used to reset debts, favors, and work pledges
seasonal_change: the town swells dramatically at harvest and tightens into reserve-counting discipline when shortages threaten.
law_context: market peace is enforced through communal law, clerk records, and family responsibility for stall, storage, and convoy conduct
public_behavior_texture: People who belong here are usually farmers, carters, grain factors, and public conduct is read through expectations such as declare loads honestly, respect family-controlled stalls and convoy order.
visible_power_signals: Ordinary decisions are visibly shaped by harvest stewards, quota clerks, patron-families.
local_tension_surface: The place feels stable only until pressures such as spoilage, price pressure, favor feuds begin showing through daily routines.
differentiator: Harvest Circle stands apart because its economy leans on grain redistribution, festival trade; public faith remains visible through unity and harvest rites are public, crowded, and used to reset debts, favors, and work pledges; outsiders are noticed fastest when they resemble cheats, panic buyers.
encounter_rhythm: Most first scenes in Harvest Circle begin through market harvest town rhythms: arrival, observation, minor correction, and only then deeper access.
